problem: Delivery relies on individual knowledge, not systems
likely_cause: Processes have never been written down — delivery quality depends on who is assigned, not how the agency is built
recommendation: Map your top 3 recurring delivery workflows end-to-end. Document them as SOPs in your PM tool. Review with the team and enforce as the default way of working within 30 days.
expected_impact: Reduced delivery errors, faster onboarding of new staff, consistent client experience
next_step: Run a workflow audit session with your team leads to identify the 3 highest-risk undocumented processes

problem: Client handoffs are ad hoc, causing dropped context and rework
likely_cause: No defined handoff protocol — information lives in email threads or people's heads, not in a shared system
recommendation: Design a standardised handoff template covering: project context, active tasks, client preferences, outstanding actions, and next milestones. Make this mandatory for any inter-team transfer.
expected_impact: Fewer errors at transition points, reduced client complaints, faster ramp-up for new account leads
next_step: Build a handoff checklist template in your PM tool this week and pilot it on one active client

problem: Processes exist but are applied inconsistently across the team
likely_cause: Documentation was created but never enforced — team falls back to individual habits under pressure
recommendation: Audit existing SOPs for completeness and usability. Identify the 2–3 most inconsistently applied workflows and run team training. Add process adherence as a standing item in your weekly team review.
expected_impact: Higher consistency, reduced manager intervention, better client outcomes
next_step: Run a 30-minute process audit with team leads to identify the biggest compliance gaps

problem: Pipeline data is unreliable or non-existent
likely_cause: No CRM in place — deals are tracked in email, spreadsheets, or not at all — making forecasting impossible and creating constant context-switching for the founder
recommendation: Implement HubSpot CRM (free tier is sufficient to start). Define 5–7 pipeline stages, assign deal owners, and mandate daily updates. Run a weekly 20-minute pipeline review from week one.
expected_impact: Improved forecast accuracy, fewer lost deals, faster sales cycle
next_step: Set up HubSpot free and migrate all active deals within 5 business days

problem: CRM exists but data quality and team adoption are low
likely_cause: The CRM was set up but never governed — no clear owner, no mandatory fields, no review rhythm to enforce use
recommendation: Run a CRM governance sprint: standardise deal stages, add 3–5 mandatory fields, assign a CRM owner, and implement a weekly pipeline review meeting. Remove stale deals older than 90 days.
expected_impact: More accurate pipeline, better handoffs between sales and delivery, reduced founder involvement in deal tracking
next_step: Audit your current CRM: count deals missing key fields and set a 2-week clean-up deadline

problem: No defined pipeline stages means deals move unpredictably
likely_cause: Sales has never been systematised — each deal follows the founder's intuition rather than a repeatable process
recommendation: Define 5–6 pipeline stages that reflect your actual sales motion (e.g. Lead → Qualified → Proposal Sent → Negotiation → Closed Won / Lost). Add entry and exit criteria for each stage. Document in your CRM and train the team.
expected_impact: Predictable pipeline movement, easier forecasting, clearer next actions on every deal
next_step: Map your last 10 closed deals and identify the 5 stages every deal went through

problem: No monthly reporting means the business is run on intuition
likely_cause: Reporting has never been formalised — data exists in multiple disconnected tools and no one owns pulling it together
recommendation: Build a single monthly business review (MBR) covering: revenue vs target, gross margin, active client count, pipeline, delivery capacity. Pull it from your PM tool, CRM, and accounting software. Review with leadership on the first working day of each month.
expected_impact: Earlier identification of margin problems, better decisions on hiring and capacity, reduced founder anxiety
next_step: Define the 5 numbers that matter most and build a simple dashboard in a spreadsheet this week

problem: No forward visibility — revenue surprises are common
likely_cause: Pipeline is not tracked consistently, or recurring revenue is not segmented from project revenue — making forward planning impossible
recommendation: Build a 90-day revenue forecast model: segment recurring retainer revenue from project revenue, apply a close rate to pipeline deals, and produce a rolling 3-month view. Update it weekly.
expected_impact: Reduced revenue shocks, better hiring timing, more confident commercial conversations
next_step: List all recurring contracts with monthly value and build a 90-day retainer baseline in a spreadsheet

problem: Reporting is inconsistent — insights are delayed or incomplete
likely_cause: Reports are produced manually and reactively rather than on a fixed cadence — data pulls are time-consuming, reducing willingness to do it regularly
recommendation: Automate your monthly report pull. Connect your PM tool, CRM, and accounting software via Zapier or Make. Create a template that auto-populates with live data. Commit to a fixed review date each month.
expected_impact: Faster insight, higher leadership confidence, more time back for the person producing reports
next_step: Identify the 3 most time-consuming parts of your current reporting and explore automation options for each

problem: Manual admin is consuming high-value team time
likely_cause: No automation has been implemented — repetitive tasks like status updates, reporting, onboarding steps, and invoice chasing are done manually by senior staff
recommendation: Start with three high-ROI automations: (1) new client onboarding workflow triggered by contract sign, (2) weekly status report auto-generated from PM tool data, (3) overdue invoice chaser via your accounting software. Use Make or Zapier to build these.
expected_impact: 3–5 hours saved per week per team member, reduced errors, faster client onboarding
next_step: Map the top 5 tasks your team does more than 3 times per week — these are your automation candidates

problem: AI tools are not being used — the agency is losing a productivity and quality advantage
likely_cause: No structured AI adoption plan — individuals may have tried tools but there are no defined use cases or team-wide workflows
recommendation: Define 5 AI use cases specific to your agency: (1) brief creation, (2) copy drafting and refinement, (3) meeting summarisation, (4) proposal structure, (5) client reporting narrative. Run a team workshop to train on these. Mandate use within defined workflows.
expected_impact: 20–40% faster content production, improved proposal quality, reduced senior time on low-value writing tasks
next_step: Run a 1-hour AI tools session with the team — identify the 3 use cases they'd find most immediately useful

problem: Automation is limited to isolated tasks — not yet embedded in core workflows
likely_cause: Early automation wins exist but there's no systematic approach — good automation is tribal, poorly documented, and fragile
recommendation: Audit existing automations: document them, assign owners, and test for reliability. Then identify the next 3 highest-value workflows to automate. Build an automation roadmap with clear business impact estimates for each.
expected_impact: More reliable workflows, compounding time savings as automation coverage increases
next_step: Create an automation register listing every current automated workflow with owner and last-tested date

problem: No operating cadence — the team is reactive and misaligned
likely_cause: Meetings happen ad hoc or not at all — there is no rhythm that keeps the team aligned on priorities, blockers, and accountability
recommendation: Implement a weekly operating cadence: (1) Monday 20-min team standup — priorities and blockers, (2) Wednesday 30-min delivery review — client health and capacity, (3) Friday 15-min leadership close — wins, pipeline, open issues. Run this for 4 weeks without exception.
expected_impact: Faster problem identification, reduced founder fire-fighting, improved team accountability
next_step: Block these three meetings in everyone's calendar this week and run the first standup on Monday

problem: Team members have no clear targets — accountability is impossible
likely_cause: KPIs have never been defined at role level — performance conversations are vague and subjective
recommendation: Define 2–3 KPIs for each key role in the agency. Keep them measurable and directly tied to business outcomes (e.g. account manager: client retention rate, upsell revenue; delivery lead: on-time delivery rate, client NPS). Review monthly in 1:1s.
expected_impact: Clearer expectations, easier performance management, more motivated team, reduced founder micromanagement
next_step: Start with the 2 most critical roles — write 3 KPIs per role and agree them with the team members this week

problem: Cadence exists but lacks consistency — accountability is patchy
likely_cause: Good intentions around structure, but meetings get cancelled under pressure and KPI reviews are skipped when things get busy
recommendation: Protect the operating cadence as a non-negotiable. Appoint a cadence owner who is not the founder. Document the agenda for each recurring meeting and enforce a no-cancel policy except for genuine emergencies.
expected_impact: Faster course correction, reduced reactive fire-fighting, improved team confidence
next_step: Identify who will own the cadence (not the founder) and give them authority to run and protect it

problem: Founder is the key constraint — growth is capped by one person's capacity
likely_cause: No delivery leads have been identified or empowered — the founder is the de facto account manager, quality checker, and escalation point for all clients
recommendation: Identify your top delivery person and formally promote them to Delivery Lead. Transfer 2 accounts to their ownership in the next 30 days. Define what 'good delivery' looks like so they can self-manage. Remove yourself from day-to-day delivery Slack channels.
expected_impact: Founder reclaims 10+ hours per week, delivery can scale beyond current capacity, business becomes sellable
next_step: Write a Delivery Lead job description and identify the internal candidate who is closest to ready

problem: All decisions route through the founder — speed and team confidence suffer
likely_cause: No documented decision rights — team defaults to asking the founder for everything because they don't know what they're empowered to decide
recommendation: Build a simple Decision Rights Matrix (RACI or DACI format). Define which decisions are made by: founder only, team lead with founder sign-off, team lead independently. Share with the full team. Review any decision that was escalated unnecessarily in your weekly leadership call.
expected_impact: Faster execution, increased team confidence, founder freed from operational noise
next_step: List the 10 decisions most frequently routed to you and categorise each as 'delegate now', 'delegate with training', or 'keep'

problem: Delegation is partial — founder still acts as safety net for too many things
likely_cause: Trust in the team has not fully developed, or team leads lack the confidence or tools to take full ownership
recommendation: Conduct a delegation audit. Map every area where you are still the last resort. For each: either build the system/process that removes the need for you, or invest in training the team lead to handle it fully. Set a 60-day target for each handover.
expected_impact: Gradual reduction in founder operational load, stronger team leads, scalable agency model
next_step: Track how many decisions were escalated to you this week — use this as your baseline

problem: Sales is unpredictable — every deal is a one-off
likely_cause: No documented sales process exists — proposals, discovery calls, and follow-ups are handled inconsistently, creating variable win rates and long cycles
recommendation: Document your sales process in 6 steps: (1) Lead qualification criteria, (2) Discovery call agenda, (3) Proposal template, (4) Follow-up sequence, (5) Negotiation limits, (6) Handoff to onboarding. Build these as reusable assets in your CRM.
expected_impact: Higher win rate, shorter sales cycle, ability to involve team in sales without re-inventing every time
next_step: Write out your ideal discovery call structure — 10 questions, time allocation, and desired outcome

problem: Pipeline is purely reactive — new business only happens when things get quiet
likely_cause: Outreach is treated as a last resort rather than a weekly habit — the agency relies on referrals and inbound with no consistent proactive activity
recommendation: Implement a weekly outreach habit: minimum 10 targeted outreach touchpoints per week (LinkedIn, email, or event follow-up). Assign a single owner. Track in CRM. Separate from delivery responsibilities so it doesn't get deprioritised.
expected_impact: More predictable pipeline, reduced revenue volatility, less panic hiring or cash flow stress
next_step: Identify who owns outreach — founder or dedicated BD — and block 3 hours per week in their calendar for it

problem: Sales process exists but lacks discipline and consistency
likely_cause: Process was built but not enforced — team skips steps under pressure, and there's no review of what's working or not
recommendation: Add a weekly pipeline review to your operating cadence. Review every active deal: stage, next action, close date, blockers. Use win/loss reviews on every closed deal to refine your process quarterly.
expected_impact: Improved close rates, faster deal velocity, better understanding of what's actually driving wins
next_step: Run a pipeline review this Friday — score every active deal on likelihood to close and identify the top 3 actions needed
